sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A Doppler ultrasound probe emits 2.0 MHz sound into a blood vessel at a 60° angle to the direction of flow. The speed of sound in tissue is approximated as 1540 m/s. For reflected ultrasound, the frequency shift is Δf = 2f0vcosθ/c. A measured shift of about 650 Hz is recorded from a region of laminar flow. Students use the relationship to estimate blood speed and interpret why the angle correction matters.
questions.stem: What blood speed corresponds to a 650 Hz Doppler shift?

explanations.D: Correct. Using Δf = 2f0vcosθ/c gives v = Δfc/(2f0cosθ). With Δf = 650 Hz, c = 1540 m/s, f0 = 2.0 × 10⁶ Hz, and cos60° = 0.5, v ≈ 0.50 m/s.
explanations.A: 0.050 m/s is too small by a factor of 10 for the given frequency shift.
explanations.B: 5.0 m/s is too large by a factor of 10.
explanations.C: 1.0 m/s would result from forgetting the factor of 2 or mishandling the angle correction.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Expected Doppler frequency shift for a 2.0 MHz probe at 60° to flow.

questions.stem: Why does the equation include cosθ?

choices.C: Only the velocity component along the ultrasound beam contributes to the shift
choices.A: Sound waves travel only in the x-direction
choices.B: The probe frequency changes with temperature only
choices.D: The cosine term converts Hz into meters per second

explanations.C: Correct. The Doppler shift depends on motion toward or away from the probe. The component of blood velocity parallel to the ultrasound beam is vcosθ, so the measured shift falls as the beam becomes more perpendicular to flow.
explanations.A: Sound can travel along the probe direction, but the issue is the velocity component along that beam.
explanations.B: Temperature can affect sound speed, but that is not why cosθ appears.
explanations.D: The cosine term is dimensionless and accounts for geometry, not unit conversion.

questions.stem: What would happen to the measured shift if the probe were oriented perpendicular to flow?

choices.C: It would equal the emitted frequency
choices.D: It would become independent of blood velocity

explanations.B: Correct. At 90°, cosθ = 0, so there is no velocity component toward or away from the probe. The Doppler shift would therefore approach zero even if blood is moving across the beam.
explanations.A: Doubling would not occur; the geometric projection becomes zero.
explanations.C: The frequency shift is much smaller than the emitted MHz frequency and does not become equal to it.
explanations.D: The Doppler shift still depends on velocity, but the measured component is zero at 90°.

questions.stem: Why is a reflected-wave Doppler shift multiplied by 2?

choices.A: The wave is shifted once when it reaches moving blood cells and again when it reflects back to the probe
choices.B: The sound speed doubles in tissue
choices.C: The probe emits two unrelated waves
choices.D: The angle is always doubled in human tissue

explanations.A: Correct. A reflected Doppler signal experiences a shift on the way to the moving scatterer and another shift as the reflected wave returns to the receiver. This produces the factor of 2 in the simplified equation.
explanations.B: The speed of sound in tissue does not double during reflection.
explanations.C: The factor is not due to two independent emitted waves.
explanations.D: The angle itself is not doubled; the two-way reflection accounts for the factor.

questions.stem: If θ were underestimated, how would the calculated blood speed be affected?

choices.D: The calculated speed would be underestimated
choices.A: The calculated speed would be overestimated
choices.B: The calculated speed would be unchanged
choices.C: The frequency shift would become negative automatically

explanations.D: Correct. If θ is underestimated, cosθ is overestimated. Since v = Δfc/(2f0cosθ), dividing by a value that is too large makes the calculated speed too small.
explanations.A: Overestimation would occur if cosθ were underestimated, not overestimated.
explanations.B: The calculated speed directly depends on the angle correction.
explanations.C: A negative shift depends on direction of motion, not simply on underestimating the angle.

questions.passage: A physics lab studies standing waves in a 0.85 m air column. When the tube is open at both ends, the fundamental wavelength is 2L. When one end is closed, the fundamental wavelength is 4L. Using the speed of sound as 343 m/s, students compare the expected fundamental frequencies for the same tube under the two boundary conditions. They observe a much lower first resonance when one end is closed.
questions.stem: What is the approximate fundamental frequency of the open-open tube?

explanations.C: Correct. For an open-open tube, λ1 = 2L = 1.70 m. The fundamental frequency is f = v/λ = 343/1.70 ≈ 202 Hz.
explanations.A: 101 Hz is the closed-open fundamental for the same tube.
explanations.B: 404 Hz is the second harmonic of the open-open tube.
explanations.D: 343 Hz is the speed of sound numerical value, not the frequency.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Expected resonance frequencies for a 0.85 m tube with different boundary conditions.

questions.stem: Why is the closed-open fundamental lower than the open-open fundamental?

choices.B: The closed-open fundamental wavelength is longer, 4L instead of 2L
choices.A: The speed of sound becomes half as large in the closed tube
choices.C: The closed end eliminates all standing waves
choices.D: The tube length doubles physically when one end closes

explanations.B: Correct. A closed-open tube has a node at the closed end and an antinode at the open end, so its fundamental wavelength is 4L. A longer wavelength at the same sound speed means a lower frequency.
explanations.A: The speed of sound in air is not halved by closing one end.
explanations.C: Closed-open tubes still support standing waves with different boundary conditions.
explanations.D: The physical tube length remains 0.85 m; the allowed wavelength changes.

questions.stem: Which harmonics are allowed in an ideal closed-open tube?

explanations.A: Correct. A tube closed at one end supports resonances at odd multiples of the fundamental: f1, 3f1, 5f1, and so on. The boundary conditions exclude even harmonics.
explanations.B: All integer harmonics occur for open-open or closed-closed tubes, not closed-open tubes.
explanations.C: Even harmonics are not allowed for an ideal closed-open air column.
explanations.D: Higher resonances exist, but only the odd harmonic series is allowed.

questions.stem: What is the frequency of the second harmonic in the open-open tube?

explanations.D: Correct. For an open-open tube, all integer harmonics are allowed. The second harmonic is 2f1 = 2(202 Hz) ≈ 404 Hz.
explanations.B: 202 Hz is the open-open fundamental, not the second harmonic.
explanations.C: 606 Hz is the third harmonic of the open-open tube.

questions.stem: What feature must exist at an open end of the air column?

choices.D: Zero air motion at all times

explanations.C: Correct. An open end allows air to move freely, so it corresponds to a displacement antinode and a pressure node. This boundary condition determines the allowed standing-wave patterns.
explanations.A: A displacement node occurs at a closed end where air cannot move.
explanations.B: Pressure antinodes occur at closed ends, not open ends.
explanations.D: Open ends permit maximal air displacement rather than zero motion.
